author: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice
book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.

metadata.author: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice

title: Canon VIII.

verses.book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
verses.text: That Hebrews ought not to be received unless they have been converted in sincerity of heart.

verses.book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
verses.text: Since certain, erring in the superstitions of the Hebrews, have thought to mock at Christ our God, and feigning to be converted to the religion of Christ do deny him, and in private and secretly keep the Sabbath and observe other Jewish customs, we decree that such persons be not received to communion, nor to prayers, nor into the Church; but let them be openly Hebrews according to their religion, and let them not bring their children to baptism, nor purchase or possess a slave.

verses.book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
verses.text: But if any of them, out of a sincere heart and in faith, is converted and makes profession with his whole heart, setting at naught their customs and observances, and so that others may be convinced and converted, such an one is to be received and baptized, and his children likewise; and let them be taught to take care to hold aloof from the ordinances of the Hebrews.

verses.book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
verses.text: But if they will not do this, let them in no wise be received.

verses.book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
verses.text: Hebrews must not be received unless they are manifestly converted with sincerity of heart.

verses.book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
verses.text: The Greek commentators Balsamon and Zonaras understood the words “nor to baptize their children” to mean, “these seeming Christians may not ‘baptize their own children,’” because they only seem to be Christians.

verses.book: The Canons of the Holy and Ecumenical Seventh Council.
verses.text: But parents were never allowed to baptize their own children, and the true sense of the words in question comes out clearly from the second half of the canon.
